Introducing Content Download

  • What is Content Download?
  • Two Methods of Content Download
  • Offering Content for Download

What is Content Download?

The Xbox Live content download service enables developers and publishers to host content on a series of content servers and then retrieve new content from their titles. The expectation is that titles will be written to accommodate new content after the title has been released to retail stores. The typical title can allow for new levels, new characters, new weapons—the possibilities are nearly endless. During your development cycle, try to plan ahead and think creatively about what would be exciting for players to download to your title a month, six months, or even a year from release. This requires that you write your title in a way that enables the easy addition of content. For example, a level-based game with death match capabilities should use a dynamically generated list of level content that is changeable by the title's content download system.

Note    Content download packages are also referred to as "offerings."

Two types of content download are available. Your content can be either free or premium. Anyone who owns your title can download free content. On the other hand, players must purchase premium content. You set the price of your premium content.

Most downloadable content is in the form of releases on Xbox Live. However, you can also release your new content on DVD.

While the content download service provides extensive security, titles should always treat downloaded content as safely as possible. Xbox Live titles must not crash as the result of incorrect data in a downloaded package. Before accessing or using any downloaded content at any time, the game title must do the following:

  1. Verify that the content is valid and unmodified by loading content signatures with XLoadContentSignatures.
  2. Compare the content against signatures calculated over the data.
  3. Update any persistent title information relating to the content.
  4. Update the download list displayed by the game's user interface to note that the content has been successfully applied.

The ContentDownload sample in the Xbox SDK demonstrates how to accomplish these tasks.

Two Methods of Content Download

The Xbox Live services provides two methods for downloading content. The first is for titles that are not Live–aware. Such titles use the Content Download Manager tool to download content from Xbox Live. The Content Download Manager is a separate XBE, named Downloader.xbe, that you can integrate into your code with minimal effort. Games restart to Downloader.xbe, which handles all of the user interface and coding required to pull down content packages from the Live service and install them on the Xbox console. When finished, Downloader reboots to the original title, which must to detect the newly installed content and use it appropriately.

The second method of providing content download is to call the XOnlineOffering family of functions to perform downloads programmatically. Although using this method is more work for you, it also offers much more flexibility. For example, in a role-playing game, a character might be able to walk up to a vendor and purchase and download a new weapon without ever leaving the game interface.

Offering Content for Download

Publishers must submit downloadable content for certification by the Xbox certification team. Once certification has approved the content, it is propagated to all of the appropriate content servers to allow for player download.

It is the publisher's responsibility to ensure that their downloadable content does not receive a higher ESRB rating than the game title that uses the content. In other words, a game title should never download and make available content that is rated higher than the title itself.

It is also the publisher's responsibility to ensure that downloadable content is safe for the game. Content should be thoroughly tested on PartnerNet before it is submitted for certification.

In addition, it is wise to take bandwidth limitations into consideration when determining the size of downloaded packages. At a conservative player bandwidth of 64 kilobits/second, over two minutes is required to download 1 MB of a game content package. While the majority of your players have a much better transfer rate than that, there is clearly an upper bound for content size—certain content just isn't viable for Internet download yet. While the exact number may change, the current upper limit for downloadable content is approximately 30 MB.
